concept: Conditional GANs solve this by feeding an extra piece of information (a class label) to both the generator and discriminator alongside the noise/image inputs.
title: Adding a Label to the Mix

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of standard GANs when generating images?

options.text: We cannot choose what specific class of image they generate.
They do not use a generator network.

exercises.explain: Standard GANs create random images from noise, which means you cannot request a specific category, like a cat or a dog.

cards.title: Ordering a Custom Picture
cards.body: Imagine telling a painter to just 'paint something.' You might get a beautiful landscape, but what if you specifically wanted a puppy? Standard GANs are like that painter—they generate amazing things, but randomly! What if we could give them a specific order?

A Conditional GAN (cGAN) is a modified GAN where both the generator and the discriminator receive an extra piece of information, like a text label. This label guides the generator to make exactly what you asked for, and helps the discriminator check if it matches the request.
cards.title: What is a Conditional GAN?

cards.body: Think of ordering food at a restaurant. In a normal GAN, the chef brings you a random dish. In a Conditional GAN, you give the waiter a ticket that says 'Spicy Noodles'. Both the chef (generator) and the quality inspector (discriminator) read that ticket to ensure you get exactly what you ordered!
cards.title: The Restaurant Ticket

cards.body: If you want a cGAN to generate handwritten digits, you feed the generator the number '7' (as a one-hot label) along with the random noise. The generator outputs an image of a 7. The discriminator then looks at both the image and the label '7' to decide if it's a realistic digit 7.
cards.title: Generating a Number 7
Both Sides Need the Label
cards.body: You might think the label is only given to the generator. But the discriminator must also receive the label! Otherwise, the generator could cheat by drawing a perfect 'dog' when you asked for a 'cat', and the discriminator wouldn't know to penalize it.

right.text: Noise + Label -> Generator -> Specific Image (Requested Category)

exercises.prompt: Where is the class label fed in a Conditional GAN?
exercises.explain: In a Conditional GAN, we feed the label to both the generator and the discriminator to ensure the image matches the requested class.

options.text: To both the generator and the discriminator.

exercises.explain: True. If the discriminator didn't see the label, it wouldn't know if the generator drew the correct category of object.
exercises.prompt: True or False: The discriminator needs the label to check if the generated image matches the requested category.
Fill in the missing concept to complete the explanation of cGANs:
exercises.sentence: To guide the generation process, we feed the generator an extra piece of information known as a class [blank].
exercises.explain: A Conditional GAN uses a label (or class information) to control the category of the generated output.

options.text: System A: A system that generates random furniture designs from noise.
System B: A system where you input the word 'chair' and it generates a chair image.

exercises.prompt: Which system demonstrates the use of a Conditional GAN?
exercises.explain: System B uses a conditional setup (labels paired with inputs) to control the style or category of output, whereas System A generates unconditionally.

options.text: The generator will stop producing realistic images altogether.
The generator could draw a cat when a dog was requested, and the discriminator wouldn't catch it.
The model becomes unsupervised and trains twice as fast.

exercises.prompt: What would happen in a Conditional GAN if the discriminator did not receive the class label input?
exercises.explain: Without the label, the discriminator only checks if the image looks real, not if it matches the requested category. We need to pass the label to both networks to enforce this condition. Next up, we will look at how we can translate images from one style to another!
